coredata.dc:title: Catechin-inspired gold nanocluster nanoprobe for selective and ratiometric dopamine detection via forming azamonardine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Ying
Liu, Yang
Zhang, Jing
Zheng, Jiaojiao
Yuan, Zhiqin
Lu, Chao
coredata.dc:description: The sensitive and selective perception of dopamine (DA, a typical neurotransmitter) is important to evaluate the biological environment. In this study, a catechin-functionalized gold nanocluster (C-Au NC) nanoprobe has been explored for the ratiometric DA sensing. The detection mechanism is based on the formation of azamonardine via selective DA-catechin chemical reaction and subsequent enhanced fluorescence emission. Using Au NC emission as the internal reference, ratiometric fluorescence variation is realized, which allows sensitive DA analysis with a limit of detection of 1.0 nM (S/N = 3) and linear response concentration range from 0 to 500 nM. The characteristic chemical reaction between catechin and DA affords favorable selectivity over other amino acids, metal ions and small molecules. In addition, the practical application of the proposed nanoprobe is validated by the accurate detection of DA content in urea and cell lysate samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Dopamine
Gold nanoclusters
Catechin
Ratiometric sensing
Azamonardine

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: DA analysis in urine and cell lysate samples
Synthesis and characterization of C-Au NCs
Evaluation of sensitivity and selectivity toward DA sensing
DA analysis in urine and cell lysate samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption spectra of Au NCs (black line), C-Au NCs (red line) and BE-catechin (blue line). (B) The FT-IR spectra of C-Au NCs (red line) and Au NCs (black line). (C) The high resolution B 1s XPS spectra of C-Au NCs. (D) Fluorescence excitation (black line) and emission (red line) spectra of C-Au NCs. (E) HRTEM image of C-Au NCs. (F) Hydrodynamic particle size distributions of Au NCs and C-Au NCs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Normalized UV–vis absorption of dopamine only (blue line), C-Au NCs without (black line) and with (red line) the addition of 5 μM DA. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-Au NCs before (black line) and after (red line) the addition of 5 μM DA. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of C-Au NCs upon adding DA with various concentrations. (B) Corresponding plots of fluorescence intensity ratio (I461/I560) versus DA concentration in the range of 0–500 nM.
Specificity of the fluorimetric assay for DA over other interferents. Fluorescence intensity ratio (I461/I560) of C-Au NCs toward various interferents in the absence (A) and presence (B) of DA.
Schematic illustration of ratiometric DA sensing using C-Au NCs as reporters.
DA detection in urine and cell lysate samples.

head.ce:title: Catechin-inspired gold nanocluster nanoprobe for selective and ratiometric dopamine detection via forming azamonardine

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Research and Development, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Research and Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Beijing Key Laboratory of Plant Resources Research and Development, Beijing Technology and Business University, Beijing 100048, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001,China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Green Catalysis Center, College of Chemistry, Zhengzhou University, Zhengzhou 450001,China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering, College of Chemistry, Beijing University of Chemical Technology, Beijing 100029, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Resource Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The sensitive and selective perception of dopamine (DA, a typical neurotransmitter) is important to evaluate the biological environment. In this study, a catechin-functionalized gold nanocluster (C-Au NC) nanoprobe has been explored for the ratiometric DA sensing. The detection mechanism is based on the formation of azamonardine via selective DA-catechin chemical reaction and subsequent enhanced fluorescence emission. Using Au NC emission as the internal reference, ratiometric fluorescence variation is realized, which allows sensitive DA analysis with a limit of detection of 1.0 nM (S/N = 3) and linear response concentration range from 0 to 500 nM. The characteristic chemical reaction between catechin and DA affords favorable selectivity over other amino acids, metal ions and small molecules. In addition, the practical application of the proposed nanoprobe is validated by the accurate detection of DA content in urea and cell lysate samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Neurotransmitters play critical roles in controlling neuron communication, which affects behavioral responses and brain functions [1]. As one of the well-known catecholamine neurotransmitters, dopamine (DA) regulates numerous biological processes, and its abnormal level is related to many diseases. For example, DA deficiency results in muscle out of control, causing aprosexia and Parkinson’s disease [2]. In contrast, over expression of DA makes energy metabolism disordered, which generally leads to Huntington’s disease [3]. To understand its biological functions and facilitate neuron-related disease diagnosis, a variety of techniques have been explored for the DA detection [4–8]. Among them, the fluorescence-based analytical methods have been developed for detecting DA with limit of detection (LOD) in tens nM level [8–12]. It is reported that DA concentrations in living systems approximately range from several to tens nM [13]. Thus, it is still appealing to explore a simple and selective analytical method for DA detection with improved sensitivity.

It is well-known that ratiometric sensing systems with built-in correction characters provide high sensitivity and accuracy [14,15]. Among them, fluorescence-based ratiometric platforms attract growing attention because of their low interference and ultrahigh sensitivity [16,17]. Fluorescent gold nanoclusters (Au NCs), consist of several to tens atoms, have been intensively used to construct ratiometric detection systems due to its tunable emission and easy surface modification characters [18–24]. For instance, highly sensitive H2O2 detection (LOD: 2.9 nM) is achieved using nanosatellite probe by integrating Au NCs and fluorescent carbon nanodots [25]. Surface functionalized Au NCs have also been applied for the ratiometric analysis of highly reactive oxygen species, microRNA and enzyme [26–28]. Despite of development of few Au NC-based DA detection systems via o-diquinone-induced fluorescence quenching [29,30], the differentiation of DA from other catechol analogues is still a challenge. Therefore, the improvement of DA perception specificity is important to develop accurate DA detection platform.

As referred in previous reports, resorcinol can react with DA to form stable and strongly fluorescent azamonardine compound [31,32]. It is thus conjectured that sensing system involving resorcinol motif would distinctly promote the detection selectivity, which is theoretically possible. In this study, we presented a resorcinol motif-contained catechin-functionalized Au NCs (C-Au NCs) nanoprobe for the DA detection. The C-Au NCs nanoprobes were prepared by chemical linking of glutathione stabilized Au NCs and catechin using 4-((2,5-dioxopyrrolidin-1-yloxy)carbonyl) phenylboronic acid (BE) as the linker. By integrating Au NCs internal reference and azamonardine formation via specific DA-catechin reaction, ratiometric and selective DA detection was achieved, the schematic illustration of DA perception mechanism was shown in Scheme 1 . With the involvement of catechin recognition site, the proposed nanoprobe exhibited ratiometric response toward DA with high sensitivity (LOD: 1.0 nM, S/N = 3). The specificity over other amino acids, small molecules and metal ions was also investigated. Moreover, the practical application of proposed C-Au NCs probe was validated by the accurate DA analysis in cell lysate samples.

Glutathione (GSH) was purchased from TCI (Shanghai, China). Chloroauric acid tetrahydrate (HAuCl4·4H2O) and 4-((2,5-dioxopyrrolidin-1-yloxy)carbonyl) phenylboronic acid (BE) were purchased from Adamas-beta (Shanghai, China). L-Serine (Ser), catechol (CT) and catechin were purchased from J&K Chemical Ltd. (Beijing, China). Potassium chloride (KCl), sodium chloride (NaCl), urea (UA), sucrose (Suc) and glucose (Glu), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), zinc chloride (ZnCl2), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and hydrochloric acid (HCl) were purchased from Beijing Chemical (Beijing, China). Dopamine (DA) was purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). L-Alanine (Ala), L-valine (Val), L-threonine (Thr), L-proline (Pro), L-methionine (Met) and L-histidine (His) were purchased from Solarbio (Beijing, China). PC12 cell and CM2-1 cell-culture medium were purchased from BeNaCulture Collection (Xinyang, China.). Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ) was obtained from a Millipore system. All chemicals were used without further purification. Phosphate buffer (PB) buffer andNaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer solutions were prepared according to the standard handbook.

Fluorescence spectra were recorded using a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained with a UV-3900H spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was obtained by using a Nicolet 6700 infrared spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). Hydrodynamic diameter measurements were performed with a Zetasizer Nano-ZS (Malvern, U.K.). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) data were collected on an ESCALAB 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with an Al Kα = 280.0 eV excitation source (Thermo, USA). High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images were performed on a JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). The time-resolved fluorescence decay curves were plotted using an FLS 980 spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, UK). Electrospray ionization mass spectroscopy (ESI-MS) was recorded in positive ESI mode using a ultrahigh-resolution time-of-flight mass spectrometry (Bruker, Germany). The Agilent 1260 Infinity HPLC analysis system coupled with a UV–visible detector were used for the acquisition and analysis of the chromatographic data, a C18 column (4.6 mm × 150 mm, 4 μm, USA) was employed for chromatographic separation. Chromatographic conditions: The mobile phase for isocratic elution consisted of MeOH-NaH2PO4 (20 mM, pH 4.0) (5:95, V/V) with the flow rate controlled at 0.5 mL⋅min−1. The detection wavelength was set at 280 nm, the column temperature was 35 °C, and the injection volume was 20 μL. Before use, the mobile phases were filtered through 0.45 μm millipore filter.

The C-Au NCs were obtained from GSH-Au NCs and catechin by using BE as the chemical linker. First, GSH-Au NCs were prepared according to previous report [33,34]. Briefly, 0.50 mL HAuCl4 (20 mM) and 0.15 mL GSH (100 mM) were mixed with 4.35 mL ultrapure water at 25 °C. The reaction mixture was heated to 70 °C under 500 rpm stirring for 24 h to yield GSH-Au NCs. After EtOH precipitation and purification, the GSH-Au NCs were solved with 10 mM PB buffer. Before catechin modification, BE-catechin complex was obtained by mixing 10 mM BE and 20 mM catechin (V/V = 2:1) in NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer/EtOH (V/V = 1:3, pH = 10) for 10 min. Then, 0.2 mL BE-catechin complex was mixed with 0.8 mL ultrapure water, and the resulted solution was added into 5.0 mL GSH-Au NCs solution with subsequent addition of 1.5 mL PB buffer, the final phosphate concentration was 10 mM. The mixture was stirred under room temperature for 12 h. After that, the solution was filtered with 3 KDa cut-off ultrafil-tration tubes to remove any unwanted reactants or byproducts to obtain C- Au NCs. The as-synthesized C-Au NCs were stored at 4 °C for subsequent characterization and assays.

A stock solution of DA (0.01 M) was first prepared, and then various concentrations were obtained by serial dilution of the stock solution. For DA detection, aliquots of different concentrations of DA were added into 1 mL of NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer (pH = 9) containing Au NCs Probe (10× dilution), in which the final concentration of total carbonate was 10 mM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm. In order to evaluate the specificity of the probe, the specificity of the metal ions, amino acids and small molecules, including Mg2+, Zn2+, CT, Ala, His, Met, Pro, Ser, Thr, Val, UA, Suc and Glu were considered. To investigate the inference, interferents were added to C-Au NCs working solutions in the absence or presence of 500 nM DA. The concentrations for metal ions, small molecules and amino acids were 10 μM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA analysis in urine and cell lysate samples
ce:section.ce:para: Urine sample analysis. Human urine samples were obtained from a healthy volunteer who was not taken any drug/DA in the past three months. Urine samples were first centrifuged, and then the supernatant is diluted to 1/20 without other pretreatment. For DA detection, 100 μL urine samples was added into 1 mL of NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer (pH = 9) containing Au NCs Probe (10× dilution), in which the final concentration of total carbonate was 10 mM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm. For conducting standard addition experiments, different concentrations of DA (2.00, 3.00 and 4.00 μM) were first added into urine samples, then 100 μL of these spiked urine samples were added into 1 mL of NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer (pH = 9) containing Au NCs Probe (10× dilution), in which the final concentration of total carbonate was 10 mM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm.

PC12 cell lysate sample analysis. The rat adrenal pheochromocytoma cell line PC12 were maintained in CM2-1 medium in a humidified atmosphere with 5% CO2 at 37 °C. The medium was replaced every day throughout the lifetime of all cultures. Cell suspension was centrifuged at 1000g for 5 min to obtain cell pellets and the cell resuspension was centrifuged again to obtain clear PC12 cell lysate, finally, then the supernatant is diluted to 1/20 for the determination of DA released from PC12 cells. For DA detection, 100 μL cell lysate sample (∼105 PC12 cells) was added into 1 mL of NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer (pH = 9) containing Au NCs Probe (10× dilution), in which the final concentration of total carbonate was 10 mM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm. For conducting standard addition experiments, different concentrations of DA (2.00, 3.00 and 4.00 μM) were first added into cell lysate samples, then 100 μL of these spiked cell lysate samples were added into 1 mL of NaHCO3-Na2CO3 buffer (pH = 9) containing Au NCs Probe (10× dilution), in which the final concentration of total carbonate was 10 mM. After 1 h reaction at 25 °C, the fluorescence emission spectra were collected by a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer at the excitation wavelength of 415 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of C-Au NCs
ce:section.ce:para: At the starting point, C-Au NCs were prepared through the chemical modification of GSH-Au NCs (Fig. S1). To promote the surface modification of catechin, BE with N-hydroxysuccinimide ester group and boric acid group was used as the chemical linker. To ensure the successful preparation of C-Au NCs, conventional characterizations including UV–vis absorption spectra, FT-IR, XPS, steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence, dynamic light scattering, and HRTEM, were performed. The UV–vis absorption spectra of C-Au NCs showed small absorption peaks around 234 and 280 nm (Fig. 1 A), which are attributed to the benzene ring from BE and catechin, respectively [35,36]. According to the FT-IR spectra (Fig. 1B), the characteristic peaks in C-Au NCs centered at 1359 cm−1 belongs to O-B stretch vibration and the characteristic peak at 860 cm−1 belongs to C-H bending vibration of benzene indicate the successful surface functionalization of catechin, and the sharper peak centered at 3436 cm−1 shows the increasing in –OH after modification. In addition, the binding energy peak around 190.40 eV in XPS spectra was assigned to B 1 s (Fig. S2 and 1C), which can be divided into two peaks (190.45 eV and 184.80 eV), suggesting the presence of B-O and B-C bonds [37]. These results revealed the successful synthesis of C-Au NCs.

The as-synthesized GSH-Au NCs showed maximum excitation and emission wavelengths centered at 415 and 560 nm, respectively (Fig. S3). It was seen that the surface modification of catechin did not change the maximum emission wavelength (Fig. 1D), implying that catechin was not involved into the electron transition of Au NCs. Interestingly, the fluorescence emission intensity also showed negligible variation, indicating the ignorable influence of catechin to the fluorescence of Au NCs. According to the time-resolved fluorescence results (Fig. S4A and S4B), the fluorescence lifetimes of Au NCs were 3.74 and 3.75 μs before and after catechin modification. This means surface catechin modification didn’t induce obvious change of fluorescence lifetime; further suggesting catechin has no effect on the electron transition of Au NCs [25].

As shown in Fig. 1E, the hydrodynamic diameter of GSH-Au NCs showed slight increment after catechin modification (from 2.7 nm to 3.6 nm), revealing the as-synthesized C-Au NCs are monodisperse. Meanwhile, the HRTEM image also demonstrated the production of monodisperse C-Au NCs with spherical size of 1.7 ± 0.3 nm and d-spacing of 0.235 nm (100 counts, Fig. 1F). The hydrodynamic diameter was slightly larger than the average size of C-Au NCs; this phenomenon is consistent with our previous works and can be attributed to existence of surface hydration layer [38]. Therefore, we can conclude that the C-Au NCs were successfully prepared.

It is reported that catechin with resorcinol motif can react with DA in a short time, which generates azamonardine compound and emits fluorescence, and resulted azamonardine structure was proved by ESI-MS spectra. As manifested in Fig. S5, a dominant peak at m/z 440.1352 is assigned for [azamonardine + H]+, suggesting the formation of azamonardine compound. The nearby peak at m/z 422.1237 can be assigned to the dehydrated azamonardine product, as shown in Fig. S6.

In this work, we aimed to propose a ratiometric nanoprobe using the prepared C-Au NCs. In consideration of the specific catechin-DA chemical reaction, we suppose that the C-Au NCs can act as nanoprobe for ratiometric and selective DA detection with high sensitivity. However, a chemical linker was used to construct C-Au NCs nanoprobe. To investigate whether catechin-DA reaction is affected by BE linker, fluorescence emission spectra of catechin-DA and BE-catechin-DA solutions were recorded. It was seen that neither fluorescence emission maximum nor intensity shows visible difference after BE linker modification (Fig. S7). This result rules out the side effect of BE linker on forming azamonardine compound. To verify the response to DA, the absorption and fluorescence profiles of the C-Au NCs in the presence of DA were first investigated. Upon adding DA, a new absorption peak centered at 415 nm was observed (Fig. 2 A), which belongs to the characteristic peak of azamonardine compound. In addition, bluish-green emission centered at 461 nm also appeared (Fig. 2B), further demonstrated the production of azamonardine compound. These results suggest the proposed C-Au NCs could be applied for ratiometric DA sensing.

It is reported that the resorcinol motif easily reacts with DA in alkaline condition [31,32], thus the effect of reaction pH was first investigated. As displayed in Fig. S8, the optimal reaction pH was found to be 9 using I461/I560 ratio as the signal reporter. However, further increased pH led to dramatic I461/I560 ratio decrease, which may be attributed to the oxidation of catechin in strong alkaline environment. As a result, all subsequent sensing experiments were conducted in pH 9.

Since the catechin is responsible to DA perception, the surface catechin density of C-Au NCs may affect the DA sensing performances. To realize sensitive DA analysis, the catechin density effect was studied by controlling the GSH/BE-catechin ratio. As shown in Fig. S9, the reaction kinetics slightly increased with the increasing GSH/BE-catechin ratio. Small surface catechin density means low molar content of catechin, which can be consumed in short time, thus causes faster kinetics. In addition, the high GSH/BE-catechin ratio resulted in large I461/I560 ratio. A possible reason is that partial catechin remains under high surface catechin density and might be oxidized in alkaline condition, which produces quinone compounds and inhibits Au NCs fluorescence. As a consequence, the I461/I560 ratio is enhanced. Notice that the I461/I560 value reached flatform after 1hr with three GSH/BE-catechin ratios, indicating the reactions complete until 1 h. The reaction rate was lower than resorcinol-DA reaction (<15 min) in previous report [39]. The concentration of resorcinol and DA were 200 μM and 10 μM, respectively. However, in this work, the total catechin of C-Au NC nanoprobe was lower than 5 μM, while the DA concentration in kinetics assays was 0.5 μM. The ultralow concentrations of reactants generally decrease the reaction rate. In addition, the surface GSH ligand may also hinder the reaction rate. As a result, the reaction kinetics in this work is slower than that of resorcinol-DA assays. To ensure the complete reaction, all fluorescence measurements were conducted after 1 h reaction. Thus, we chose the GSH/BE-catechin ratio of 15, pH 9 and 1 h reaction time for the construction of ratiometric DA sensing platform.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of sensitivity and selectivity toward DA sensing
ce:section.ce:para: Introducing DA into C-Au NCs solution yielded noticeable increment of bluish-green emission, while the orang emission was nearly unchanged. The strong fluorescence and dual-emission characters indicated the C-Au NCs could as a promising nanoprobe for ratiometric DA sensing. For evaluating the sensitivity, fluorescence emission spectra of C-Au NC nanoprobe solution after addition of DA with different concentrations were recorded with 1 h reaction. As manifested in Fig. 3 A, the characteristic emission peak centered at 461 nm belongs to azamonardine structure gradually increased as increasing DA concentrations from 0 to 500 nM. In contrast, the characteristic emission peak centered at 560 nm belongs to Au NCs remained, suggesting the ideal role of internal reference with this orange emission. As a result, I461/I560 ratio can be explored as the signal to indicate the DA concentration. The I461/I560 value exhibited a good linear relationship (R 2 = 0.995) versus the DA concentration ranging from 0 to 500 nM (Fig. 3B), and the linearity could be well described as an equation: I461/I560 = 0.10083 + K[Q], where K is the fluorescence enhancement constant, and [Q] is the concentration of DA. K was calculated to be 1.7 × 106 M−1 by linear regression of the plots, and the LOD was calculated to be 1.0 nM (S/N = 3). Such a LOD is much lower than that of some reported nanoprobes (Table S1) [7,8,10,39–44]. The dual-emission character and rapid DA-catechin reaction majorly respond for the excellent sensitivity. To understand the reproducibility of proposed nanoprobe, 10 repeated measurements of C-Au NC solution fluorescence response-induced by 500 nM DA were conducted. As illustrated in Fig. S10, the low relative standard deviation (2.5%) of I461/I560 ratio proved the highly reproducible character of the designed C-Au NC nanoprobe. As is known, catechin is easily oxidized under alkaline conditions or catalyzed by enzymes or oxidants [45,46]. As a polyphenol, the oxidation reaction of catechin usually occurs in the o-diphenol position. However, the o-diphenol of catechin was connected with BE linker, as shown in Fig. S1. In this case, the oxidation trend of catechin is partially inhibited by active site blocking, leading to high stability of proposed nanoprobe. To further verify the accuracy of DA detection, HPLC characterization with UV detector was conducted through standard curve method (Fig. S11). The DA signal was obtained with a retention time of 4.006 min (Fig. S12). In consideration of low sensitivity of UV spectrophotometer, HPLC can only detect DA in mM level. As shown in Table S2, the measured DA concentrations in spiked samples with our proposed C-Au NCs nanoprobe were consistent with that using HPLC, indicating the accuracy of established ratiometric DA sensing platform.

In order to inspect whether the increased bluish-green emission and I461/I560 ratio induced by DA is specific; the fluorescence emission spectra of C-Au NC nanoprobe solution with the addition of possible interferents were measured. For selectivity evaluation, small molecules, metal ions, and amino acids, including CT, Suc, Glu, UA, Zn2+, Mg2+, Ala, His, Met, Pro, Ser, Thr, and Val were investigated under the same conditions. The concentrations for small molecules, metal ions, and amino acids were all 10 μM, respectively. The concentrations of all possible interferents were 20-folds higher than DA. As displayed in Fig. 4 A, only DA induced distinct enhancement of I461/I560 ratio, while none of these interferents could cause a conspicuous fluorescence variation as 500 nM DA did. Moreover, the DA sensing was not affected even with the presence of these interferents (Fig. 4B). These results indicated that only DA can selectively enhance the fluorescence by forming azamonardine structure. Interestingly, the specificity of chemical reaction on Au NC surface was generally the same as in solution phase (Fig. S13). The unchanged specificity and good tolerance further demonstrated the possibility of practical DA analysis in biological samples or other complicated environment. Taken together, the favorable sensitivity and selectivity imply that C-Au NC nanoprobe provides a platform for the ratiometric and efficient DA perception.

ce:section.ce:section-title: DA analysis in urine and cell lysate samples
ce:section.ce:para: To further demonstrate the practical application of the proposed platform, we tried to detect DA in urine and cell lysate samples. The summary of DA detection in urea and cell lysate samples was listed in Table 1 . The DA in urine samples was not detected. The accuracy was verified by the satisfying recoveries (99.6–103.8%) and relative standard deviation (RSD, 0.9–4.7%) by a standard addition method. In addition, the potential for practical DA analysis was further proved by cell lysate tests. As shown in Table 1, the detected DA in untreated PC12 cell lysate were not founded, and the favorable recoveries (99.3–104.1%) and RSD values (2.9–4.5%) in cell lysate samples also suggest the practical application of the proposed DA detection platform. In a word, the established C-Au NC-based ratiometric platform is capable of DA sensing in complicated biological media.

In summary, catechin-modified Au NC nanoprobe for ratiometric DA sensing has been explored by integrating Au NC internal reference and catechin-DA reaction. The self-calibration character makes the nanoprobe sensitive and accurate. Meanwhile, the specific catechin-DA chemical reaction also endows the as-prepared nanoprobe favorable selectivity toward DA over other amino acids, small molecules or metal ions. Furthermore, the practical DA analysis in urea and cell lysate samples is achieved using the proposed C-Au NC nanoprobe. This work not only demonstrates a ratiometric neurotransmitter sensing system, but also proves the effective chemical reaction on nanostructure surface. Thus, new avenues for the design of versatile methods for the rapid and selective perception of signal transmitters and biomarkers by combining nanomaterials and specific chemical reactions are now possible in medicine or life-science related fields.

Ying Liu: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Yang Liu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation. Jing Zhang: Software, Writing – review & editing. Jiaojiao Zheng: Software, Data curation. Zhiqin Yuan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing. Chao Lu: Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the Beijing Natural Science Foundation (2202038), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (22074005 and 21974008), the Open Research Fund Program of Beijing Key Lab of Plant Resource Research and Development, Beijing Technology and Business University (PRRD-2021-YB6).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2022.121142.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: On the role of volume transmission and receptor–receptor interactions in social behaviour: Focus on central catecholamine and oxytocin neurons

Leo, et al., On the role of volume transmission and receptor–receptor interactions in social behaviour: Focus on central catecholamine and oxytocin neurons, Brain Res, 1476 (2012) 119-131.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Dopamine neurons derived from embryonic stem cells function in an animal model of Parkinson's disease

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent sensor array for separation-free dopamine analogue discrimination via polyethyleneimine-mediated self-polymerization reaction
